BTW, I thought you were a victim because you were also yelled at, I guess I was wrong though...

^^^You simply have to enter the roleplay's signup sheet, and once the creator of the roleplay officially starts the roleplaying, you enter lines that you would like your OCs to say and interact with other people's OCs. I recommend being involved with plot points as they create quite the twist when your OC is also associated with them. 
As for your OC, your OC's personalities, actions, style, etc. will always develop as you interact with other roleplayer's OCs and you add detail to your character's dialogue, actions, etc. 
Your format should most likely be like this: [Your OC] said, "[Dialogue]" [Any actions your OCs do or any details you would like to add in about them. It's also fine if you add detail for other people's OCs that your OC is interacting with. Just nothing too extreme, and if it involves you acting out a lot of the other OC's role, ask permission.]
Feel free to add any details about your OC as you go along or, you can also for example, show your OC's past during your roleplaying if you wish.
In the place/post where you roleplay, if you aren't entering a dialogue/action for your character or something based on this roleplay, always put parentheses or do // at the end or anything to show that this is not your character/OC roleplaying right now, you are mentioning something unrelated.
That's all of my pointers and basically all for the roleplaying rules I keep in mind of(The main ones and helpful ones^^^) . I hope this helps and my apologies if this ended up too long(or if you already know all this and only asked for some pointers)
@Stargazer369 I pinged you because you're obv the roleplay queen and anything I missed out on my veryyy long rules/pointers paragraph?^^^<333

title: Losing Game - Chapter One
content:
Check out the prologue for some context haha

————

“How much do you think she’ll recall?”
The woman’s voice was heavy with worry.
“I’ve never seen something like this, Minerva. I can’t know,” replied a man’s cold sound.
The girl blinked open her hazel eyes. The room was small, with time-weathered wallpaper that showed blue flowers on a white background. She laid upon a metal-framed bed with threadbare white sheets.
“She’s awake!” A woman rushed to the girl’s bedside, long grey hair sweeping the rigid lines of her face. “Dear, tell me your name.”
Vian.
She gave a startled blink as the name came to her head. It felt wrong— she wasn’t Vian, whoever he was. So why was the name ringing in her head?
“A—”
Vian. More insistent this time. What was her name, actually? Azalea? Alyssa? Alina? Alina felt right. Not perfect, but close. Alina. Sure.
“A—”
VIAN.
“Avia,” she croaked, catching herself off-guard. She’d decided on Alina.
The woman took no note. “Avia. How are you feeling?”
“Who are you?”
Another figure stepped forward, tall and severe, with gaunt features and platinum-blonde hair. “I believe Headmistress McGonagall asked you a question.”
The girl— Alina? Avia?— sat up abruptly. “No. No, I’ve been in this damned room for who knows how long. I’m asking questions. What is happening?”
The woman, McGonagall, sighed heavily. “You were an unfortunate victim of a magical accident. A dying wizard attempted a piece of dark magic he did not nearly have the talent for, and you are the product.” She shot a sharp look at the man beside her. “Professor Gauldint would’ve seen you killed, but I suggested otherwise. If you possess any sort of magical ability, you could be valuable. If not, you’ll have your memories wiped and you may resume your Muggle activities.”
“Muggle? Magic? I’m not following,” Avia choked. “What are you trying to say?”
McGonagall drew a slender piece of wood from her pocket. “Try this. It’s a training wand— simple, made to channel any fragment of magic. It’d be incredibly difficult to do much damage with it.” She held the wand to Avia.
Avia took it with shaking hands, her knuckles white as she held it. A warm sensation rushed through her veins, up her arm, into her chest, and it was as though a flood gate had been opened. The wand caught fire, flames raging along her skin, scorching her sleeves. Professor Gauldint drew a long silver wand and aimed it at her. Upon the man’s yell of “Aguamenti!”, the raging blaze consuming her was extinguished. Avia sat in the burnt, drenched mess of smoking sheets. Her skin was completely unmarked, though the training wand had most definitely seen better days.
McGonagall looked thoroughly shook. She turned to Gauldint. “He was powerful,” she whispered, “but that powerful? What did he do to create her?”
“Create me? I’m— I want to say I’m somewhere around fifteen— I don’t think this accident made me, per say…”
Gauldint shook his head. “You were a Muggle. Your magic had to come from somewhere. Like it or not, the accident made you what you are now. A witch.”
“Sorry, witch? What exactly do you mean by tha—”
But Gauldint and McGonagall had moved on. “Do we enroll her?” The headmistress asked, her strain obvious. “This amount of power has to be harnessed somehow.”
“Do we have other options?” Gauldint sighed. “We’re not putting a sixteen-year-old in a class of first-years, though.”
“Well, put her down as a fifth-year. We’ll sort her seperately and slot her into the existing class.”
“Sixteen— hold on, how long have I been in this Godforsaken room?”
Gauldint grit his teeth. “We did some medical examinations yesterday while you were under. Our healers revealed that you turned sixteen a matter of days ago. It’s August twenty-ninth.”
Avia’s head was reeling. “June,” she whispered. “I remember that— June was when the accident happened. So why did no one tell me anything? Why’d you leave me alone in here for months without a word? I tried to take the door off the hinges, tried to break the window and almost broke my hand instead. Did no one care?”
“Of course we cared.” McGonagall shook her head. “Gauldint, go get the Sorting Hat.”
As the platinum-haired man left, opening the padlocked door with nothing but a flick of his wand, McGonagall helped Avia to her feet. “Things were complicated, Avia. The wizard you saw lose his life made a grave mistake. He was not a dark wizard, nor did he have the abilities to become one. He was a good-hearted man. What could’ve driven him to attempt dark magic, I can’t say. But it went awry. I’m sorry, my dear, for the mess this has landed you in.” She offered an attempt at a smile. “On the bright side, you’re going to be enrolled in a school— the school which I lead— that can teach you how to overcome your circumstances and master this power. Hogwarts, it’s called. You’ll be given a house with like-minded students. Gryffindor, house of the brave. Hufflepuff, house of the loyal. Ravenclaw, house of the wise, or Slytherin, house of the cunning. Your house will be something of a family to you. I’ll personally see to it that you are taken to Diagon Alley— it’s a wonderful place, really, for you to buy books, robes, and your own wand and owl.” She patted Avia’s shoulder. “I know it’s a lot to take in, but you’ll find yourself at home in no time.”
Gauldint returned with a weathered, slightly singed hat. It had a crumpled brown point and had clearly seen better days. McGonagall took it from him and addressed the hat directly.
“We’ll need to skip the fanfare,” she informed it. “Sort the girl.”
“If I must,” replied the hat.
Replied the hat.
The hat was talking.
In the last half-hour, Avia had forgotten her own name, set herself on fire, witnessed magic, and heard a hat speak. She was losing it.
McGonagall placed the hat over her head, and only then did it get weirder. The hat spoke once more, but not aloud. In her head. She could hear its voice thrumming in her skull.
Hmm. Hmm, yes… Slytherin? Perhaps… though, I sense something familiar. You are a Ravenclaw, aren’t you? You’re not Avia, for there is no such girl, but whatever you are, you are a Ravenclaw. I have sorted you before.
Vian. The blasted voice was at it again— the soft tone of a young man, insistent. You have sorted me. Vian. I am a Ravenclaw. At last, someone who recognizes me.
The hat paused. But, Vian, this is no longer your life. Avia, or whoever you are, you are no Ravenclaw. Slytherin, I see ambition. But I see something just in you. You value hard work, do you not? And you will work. You will work, indeed… in Gryffindor.
Apparently the last word had not, in fact, been in her head, as McGonagall clapped. “Gryffindor! I was a Gryffindor, back in my Hogwarts days.” She retrieved the Sorting Hat. “You ought to rest, Avia. Tomorrow we’re getting you out of the staff housing. Tomorrow, you’re off to Diagon Alley.”
Hours later, the room was dark and abandoned. Avia lay in bed, her mind racing. Witch. Diagon Alley. Hogwarts. All these made-up words that were suddenly seeming a lot less made-up. She considered attempting to rationalize it, a thought which she quickly dismissed. She’d made up her mind.
Avia would go to the wizard school. She’d pretend to have half a clue of what was going on. And she wouldn’t rest another minute until she knew who Vian was.
The voice in the back of her mind spoke up again.
Sweetheart, you already do.

Jacelyn stood in the lobby, a small brown leather bag at her feet. She looked around and saw a pretty girl, watching her from a dimly lit corner. Jacelyn smiled softly and dipped her head in a respectful hello.

I...can actually kind of see Draco possibly having some kind of feelings for Hermione. It does seem like he was always fixated on her, and singling her out quite often (certainly, bullies will do that with their targets, and Draco was a bully towards her, but maybe there was a bit more to his fixation on her than may have been obvious?)
The things he said to her and his behaviour is certainly not defensible, and I'm not trying to make excuses for it, but he may have been in conflict with and possibly troubled by the fact he had feelings for a "m**b***d" (using this word in the context of Draco and his family's viewpoint) (and a member of Gryffindor House, at that!), and some of his behaviour was at least partly a cover-up, of sorts. (I think there is still a good chance there wasn't anything there, and he was just being a jerky bully, but the theory he did experience feelings for Hermione is one I don't feel like I can entirely dismiss, either)
I certainly don't ship "Dramione", and don't think there was any kind of reciprocal feelings from Hermione towards Draco, if he indeed had feelings.

Ooo yeah something else I thought of that is about the twisteds. I heavily hc all the twisteds to have, like, really poor vision. Like for the most part 90% of the twisteds can't see much more than blurry dots, with basically every Twisted being some type of nearsighted, although I dunno if this is a hc because it is sorta implied with the Perfect Christmas song.
This nearsightedness is combined with poor object permanence, which is likely a result of MOTHER controlling them.

And with the human eyes, I did have the lil diagram but in case it's not clear, the pupil literally eats the iris, entirely erasing it. That also probably makes their vision pretty poor too lol.

Also someday I plan to remake Twisted Arthur (Not his design or basic gimmick though, just adding onto it.) In this AU Twisted Arthur is especially blind, being unable to see toons unless they are moving (This is a combination of stress, the poor twisted vision, the fact he has to see out of a CRT, and the fact that Arthur already had vision problems before (Since he canonically needs reading glasses)) he can kinda see better when he's not being controlled, but he still has a hard time.

Az has more control, she seems excellent at working her opponents up with insults and jabs so they lose their footing and become sloppy, but she also seems to be way to overconfident. She's lost so much, and because of that, worked herself to become strong enough to never lose anything ever again, and that seems to show. Mercedes is also far to confident, but she did seem to fall for Az's taunts, returning her own, and openly starting the fight. Even going to burn Az for just a couple words. This'll be interesting :D) 
(I'm going to talk about Arryn and Erjon in an outside view. I want to try something new ^^) 
@XxSonar18GrangerxX @Stargazer369 
Arryn and Erjon seemed to be having a very rapid, very intense conversation. The former had his arms crossed, while the latter raised his hands in the universal sign of exasperation. Neither of them were focused on the fight anymore, the sounds of shouting and the smell of burning flesh fading into the background. 
"Arryn, this is serious. How many times do I have to tell you-" Erjon dragged a tattooed hand down his face, and took a slow breath. "I'm worried. You've never been like this." Arryn frowned at his brother's words, and snapped back, "Like what? Like I'm willing to put down my life for someone that isn't you or our High Lord and Lady?" A sliver of shadow slipped from inside his wrist, hovering in the air beside him. It looked like a ribbon, but those sharp edges were curved enough to slice if the Shadowsinger pleased. "I've lost too much, to much, and I'm not living through that again. Not when I found my-" He cut himself off, but Erjon just smirked. 
"I knew it." 
"Knew what, you cauldron-blasted busybody?" 
"She's your mate." 
Arryn's frown deepened, but he didn't deny it. His brother shook his head slowly. "Does she know?" The Shadowsinger shook his head, and it was Erjon's turn to frown.    "Why haven't you told her? Azalea deserves to know. When she figures it out..." Arryn sighed, running a shaking hand through his dark hair, and whispered, "She's not the only one who is still living in the pain of her past. She would see it as a shackle, and I can't add another link onto the chain she's already dragging behind her." He swallowed, wings drooping against his back, the shadows that had been collecting around him pooling at his feet. "That's why I said what I said. It's the only way she can finally be free. She isn't meant for- for this." Erjon paused, then nodded, his own wings flaring wide before snapping to his back. "I understand, but even though she's your mate, you can't assume you know what she needs. I did that, and almost got turned to ashes." He chuckled, then clapped his brother on the shoulder. "Tell her. Do what you have to do, then ask her what she would want. It's for the best." With that, Arryn was left alone, leaning against a tree while he watched his brother begin stacking their packs. He turned his gaze to the fight and immediately launched forward. His face was cold. There were no emotions except the cruel anger lighting his brown eyes. Shadows began to float up from the puddle at Arryn's feet, forming into tiny pinpoints, and with a single blink, he had them aimed at the female utop what his blood, what the bond, considered his female. 

(For context, the mating bond, if not properly accepted, can cause the male to do insane things to win the female's affection. It goes back to the time when they were little more than beasts, always fighting for their next meal and a chance to breed and survive. Azalea didn't know about the bond she hadn't accepted, which was why seeing her pinned beneath Mercedes, burns decorating her skin, sent Arryn into a frenzy. Erjon would not feel the same, given Mercedes had accepted the bond, and he could think outside of the urges. He knew his mate could fend for herself, but his brother had yet to see that. He can't see outside of his bloodlust for anything hurting his future partner, and that is what was happening. )

Aryyn swallowed, his fingers white against the rim of his glass, and he was shaking slightly- he could feel it- with restraint. Restraint against what, he wasn't exactly sure, but all them same, he should get a medal for his willpower. "I don't expect you to fall apart, I expect you to keep me at a distance, because that is what makes this want so much sweeter." He took a step toward her, then a step back, a growl sounding from his throat. "What? Do you not like what you see? Is it surprising to you that I might- that I-" He raked a hand through his hair, squeezing his eyes shut, and taking another step back. "No point in chasing the stars when the sun's rising." He said softly, a hint of regret in his words, and then he turned away, not wanting to burden her with feelings that weren't so fleeting anymore. That obviously weren't reciprocated. @XxSonar18GrangerxX
